responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn parasomnia unit navigates three core tensions: (1) ecological validity versus safety—a residential setting captures naturalistic behavior but demands rigorous hazard control; (2) event yield versus operational efficiency—infrequent episodes require extended stays and redundant monitoring, straining budget and staffing; (3) clinical-grade capture versus real-world sustainability—full PSG is gold-standard but labor-intensive annotation limits throughput. The selected levers address recruitment sustainability under realistic yield assumptions, sensor-tier trade-offs that balance data richness against technician burden, staffing models that protect safety margins while controlling personnel costs, phased facility expansion tied to measurable validation milestones, and governance structures that enable course correction without destabilizing the program. These levers jointly determine whether the unit achieves its three aims or fractures under operational strain.

levers.name: Recruitment Entry Pathway and Diagnostic Stringency
levers.consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission screening (specialist interview + prior PSG + structured exclusion checklist) reduces on-site misadmission and participant frustration, but delays enrollment by 4–6 weeks per cohort. Systemic: A 2–week gating period increases staffing utilization (fewer wasted bed-nights on misclassified participants) and improves event yield from ~65% to ~78%, reducing the unproductive-admission ceiling from 20% to ~12%; this shifts 3–5 participants per year from zero-event discharges to event-generating admissions. Strategic: Frontloading diagnostic certainty enables rapid scientific publications and boosts grant-review credibility, but slower enrollment may compress year-two cohort recruitment, creating calendar risk if DFG funding is delayed.
options: Implement a two-stage pathway with a mandatory videoconsult and prior-PSG review before formal enrollment, scheduling only confirmed candidates to minimize on-site diagnostic ambiguity.

Accept clinic referrals with self-reported parasomnia history and perform diagnostic confirmation during the first 3 nights in-residence, discharging participants who lack captured events by day 4 and recycling those bed-slots to reduce vacancy.

Recruit 40% of participants from the Deutsche Gesellschaft network using a distributed phone-interview checklist administered by regional neurologists, circumventing University Hospital bottlenecks and enabling parallel enrollment pipelines.
levers.review_lever: Controls diagnostic precision vs. enrollment velocity. Weakness: The options fail to account for bed-partner availability as a confounding variable—participants lacking corroborating household history may have legitimate parasomnias but be systematically excluded, biasing the final cohort toward cohabiting individuals.

levers.name: Tiered Sensing Architecture and Technician Annotation Load
levers.consequences: Immediate: Nightly dry-electrode EEG headbands + mattress sensors + infrared cameras generate 500+ GB per participant per week; dual-rater manual annotation of all continuous data requires 8–12 hours per night of technician/rater time, bottlenecking Aim 3 algorithm development. Systemic: Deploying scheduled enhanced PSG only on nights 1, mid-stay, and final (plus escalation nights) reduces total PSG montage-nights to ~30% of stay-nights, cutting annotation labor by ~55%, but this sparse sampling may miss infrequent event morphology variants, lowering algorithm generalization by an estimated 12–18%. Strategic: Conservative full PSG saturates the annotation pipeline and delays algorithm publication by 6+ months; adaptive sparse PSG accelerates time-to-publication but risks underfitting the event-triage model to rare phenotypes not captured on scheduled nights.
options: Acquire continuous full PSG on all nights for the first 15 participants (pilot cohort), generating complete annotation dataset for algorithm training, then transition to sparse PSG for subsequent cohorts once baseline morphology is characterized.

Implement nightly low-burden monitoring with structured human review flags: technician-tagged candidate windows undergo dual-rater PSG-free analysis (visual scoring from video + headband EEG + ambient audio), advancing to full PSG only when inter-rater agreement reaches <80%.

Deploy a trained graduate student to conduct real-time semi-automated triage on headband EEG and video streams each night, automatically triggering full PSG montage when spectral/movement signatures match prototypical parasomnia patterns, with human review reserved for algorithm-uncertain events.
levers.review_lever: Controls annotation labor vs. phenotypic completeness. Weakness: The options fail to distinguish between annotation cost for Aim 2 (phenotyping) and Aim 3 (algorithm development)—a sparse PSG schedule may suffice for characterization but starve the algorithm of training examples, creating a hidden trade-off between publication speed and model robustness.

levers.name: Staffing Model and Night Coverage Redundancy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Covering 8 active suites nightly requires a minimum of 3 technicians on rotating 5-day shifts to ensure safe response to events; a full-time headcount of 4 technicians provides a 33% safety margin and absorbs vacation/illness. Systemic: Staffing a 3-person core with on-call backup shifts annual personnel costs by ~€280K (vs. 4-person core), but on-call deployment adds ~15% overtime and reduces annotation consistency (on-call staff are less familiar with participants and sensor-system quirks), lowering inter-rater reliability by an estimated 4–6 percentage points. Strategic: Lean staffing (3 FTE) preserves budget headroom for algorithm development and expansion; redundant staffing (4 FTE) ensures consistent data quality and reduces burnout-driven staff turnover, but forecloses year-three expansion if DFG funding plateaus.
options: Hire a full-time 4-person technician pool with formal night-shift rotation (5 nights per week per technician, ~2 weekends per month), eliminating on-call dependency and establishing continuity; absorb the €280K cost by reducing participant compensation from €80 to €65 per night or accepting deferred expansion.

Deploy a core 3-person team supplemented by a rotating pool of 2 trained undergraduate research assistants paid hourly at €16/hour, scheduled on-call for events; delegate annotation to a separate 2-person dedicated scoring cohort working day-shift, decoupling night response from daytime analysis.

Establish a formal collaboration with University Hospital Bonn's sleep technician training program, embedding 1–2 graduate-level students in the unit for 6-month rotations, providing them credit toward their clinical certification while supply-chaining technician labor at zero marginal cost after initial training.
levers.review_lever: Controls staffing stability vs. personnel budget. Weakness: The options fail to address the hidden cost of turnover—a lean on-call model may incur 20–30% annual technician replacement, driving retraining costs and loss of institutional knowledge that exceeds the apparent budget savings.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Gating and Capacity Planning
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deferring expansion from 8 to 12 suites until month 12–18 (after pilot validation) reduces pre-operational capital risk; the 4-suite buffer provides insurance against design flaws without overbuilding. Systemic: Maintaining 8 suites for year one enrolls ~20 participants at 70-day average stay; adding 4 suites in month 18 increases year-two capacity to ~35 participants (a 75% increase), aligning with the year-two budget of €1.2M. However, premature expansion to 12 suites in month 6 could increase year-two enrollment to ~50–55 participants, improving event yield statistics by ~22%, but risks property-lease penalties and renovation cost overruns if quality issues emerge. Strategic: Conservative expansion minimizes sunk costs and preserves strategic optionality; aggressive expansion front-loads data collection and accelerates publication timeline, but betting infrastructure capital on unproven operational assumptions may consume the year-three conditional budget prematurely.
options: Gate expansion to month 18, conditional on achieving 80% data-quality score (inter-rater PSG reliability >85%, <8% false-alarm rate, <10% unproductive admissions); fund 4-suite renovation from year-two budget only if milestones are met.

Initiate 4-suite build-out in month 6 using a deferred-payment lease structure, deferring full occupancy to month 12; this front-loads infrastructure cost (~€180K) but enables gradual staffing ramp and captures 5–10 additional participants in year two.

Partner with University Hospital Bonn to lease 4 clinical sleep-medicine beds for overflow monitoring during peak enrollment windows (e.g., months 12–18), avoiding permanent facility expansion and maintaining flexibility to pivot if event yields prove lower than 70%.
levers.review_lever: Controls capital risk vs. enrollment momentum. Weakness: The options fail to model the interaction between facility size, staff familiarity, and data-quality consistency—a rapid expansion to 12 suites may dilute technician expertise across more participants, degrading inter-rater reliability and offsetting the enrollment gain.

levers.name: Data Sharing and Repository Alignment Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Depositing de-identified physiological data (EEG, EOG, EMG, actigraphy, ambient sensors) in a BIDS-compliant repository (OpenNeuro or PhysioNet) enables secondary analysis and algorithmic benchmarking by external groups. Systemic: Public data sharing attracts ~15–25 external downloads per dataset per year and typically generates 2–4 citation-linked downstream publications within 24 months; this amplifies research impact (estimated 1.5–2× citation multiplier) but requires 80–120 hours of documentation effort per publication cycle and raises de-identification risk if sensor streams contain subtle privacy leakage (e.g., acoustic signatures revealing identity). Strategic: Open-data positioning strengthens grant renewals and establishes the unit as a community resource, but bedside-video privacy constraints limit the most naturalistic modality, potentially undermining algorithm generalization if external groups train on physiology alone.
options: Deposit complete physiological datasets (EEG, video-free) in OpenNeuro under CC0 licensing, with full BIDS metadata and annotated event csvs, enabling external algorithm benchmarking and maximizing citations; budget 100 hours year two for documentation.

Implement a tiered access model: de-identified physiology available under CC-BY open license, but video-linked datasets (optical flow, pose estimates without facial pixels) restricted to Data Use Agreements; this preserves privacy while enabling external algorithm development on a reduced feature set.

Restrict public sharing to an annual 'curated release' containing 10–15 fully annotated, de-identified participant records, managing privacy risk and documentation burden while still supporting 2–3 external collaborations per year through direct Data Use Agreements.
levers.review_lever: Controls data access vs. privacy surface. Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory landscape—DPA compliance and GDPR Article 89 (research exemptions) require legal review regardless of repository choice, adding 8–10 weeks of upfront work that is not budgeted in the team's timeline.

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development Scope and Clinical Deployment Ambition
levers.consequences: Immediate: A benchmarking-only algorithm (Aim 3 as designed) produces sensitivity/specificity metrics against dual human scoring, enabling peer-reviewed publication in methods journals and reducing manual review burden from 8 hours/night to ~3 hours/night (by prioritizing probable events). Systemic: Advancing to a 'clinical-deployment-ready' algorithm (prospective validation against clinician interpretation, internal checklist review) demands 200+ additional hours of validation work and a 6–month extension beyond month 36, but increases algorithm adoption by hospital sleep labs, generating €50–150K in consultation revenue by year four. However, overstatement of algorithm maturity invites regulatory scrutiny and reputational risk if external groups benchmark the model and report failures on underrepresented phenotypes. Strategic: Conservative benchmarking-only scope aligns with the early-stage research mission and avoids premature clinical claims; deployment-ready framing could accelerate pharmaceutical partnerships (raising year-three revenue by ~€200K), but risks mission creep and regulatory entanglement that compromises academic independence.
options: Limit Aim 3 to a benchmarking study: train a semi-automated event triage on year-one/two data, report sensitivity/specificity against dual human scorers, and publish in a sleep medicine methods journal; explicitly avoid clinical-deployment language and recommend human oversight for any downstream use.

Develop the algorithm with prospective validation on a held-out test cohort (final 15–20 participants), comparing system rankings against clinician consensus, and submit a separate clinical-validation manuscript; position as 'promising for research use' and offer it freely to academic sleep labs as a screening tool.

Engineer the algorithm for ease-of-use deployment in hospital PSG systems via a DICOM plugin and REST API, document validation against multiple cohorts, and license it to sleep-medicine software vendors for a milestone-based revenue share; this accelerates algorithm reach but introduces vendor dependencies and marketing pressures that may dilute academic publishing.
levers.review_lever: Controls scientific scope vs. translational ambition. Weakness: The options fail to address the Goodhart's Law problem—once an algorithm is positioned as a decision-support tool, institutional use will shift from 'research benchmarking' to 'clinical triage,' inverting the incentive structure and eroding the algorithm's transparency.

levers.name: DFG Funding Contingency and Year-Three Sustainability Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Primary funding from DFG grants covers €3.5M of the €3.8M budget; a €300K internal University of Bonn allocation funds contingency and year-three conditional costs. Systemic: If DFG funding is delayed beyond month 3 or reduced below €2.8M, year-one operations must be sustained by University internal funds, which are typically reallocated annually and cannot be relied upon; a delay of 6+ months forces a 25% reduction in pilot enrollment (from 20 to 15 participants) or a 4-month slippage to month 7 start date, cutting year-one capture by ~2–3 events and deferring publication to month 30. Strategic: Securing pharmaceutical partnerships (optional per plan) as supplementary revenue (€150–300K per partner) de-risks year-three expansion and funds algorithm commercialization; however, dependence on pharma revenue incentivizes faster-than-prudent deployment and dilutes the unit's independence.
options: Frontload all discretionary spending (equipment procurement, participant recruitment marketing) into months 1–3 using committed internal funds, demonstrating operational viability before DFG funds arrive; this compresses cash-flow risk into a 90-day window.

Negotiate a formal letter of intent with the DFG program officer at month −3 (pre-submission), securing a binding funding commitment or a written contingency timeline; if DFG approval is delayed >4 months, activate a secondary application to European Sleep Research Society infrastructure grants.

Cultivate 2–3 pharmaceutical partnerships in parallel (e.g., orexin antagonists, antiepileptic candidates for parasomnia) by month 6, structuring contracts to yield €100–150K each by month 18 in exchange for observational outcome reporting (non-exclusive, with full academic publication rights); use pharma revenue to de-fund year-three contingency and preserve University resources.
levers.review_lever: Controls institutional dependency vs. external alignment. Weakness: The options fail to model the reputational cost of pharmaceutical partnerships—even with retained publication rights, external perception of industry sponsorship may bias recruitment (participants may self-select for higher treatment expectations) and bias interpretation (reviewers may discount findings as industry-influenced).

responses.summary: The seven levers are well-balanced across operational domains (recruitment, sensing, staffing, facility, data, algorithm, and funding), each confronting a distinct core tension inherent in residential parasomnia research. Strengths: recruitment gating addresses yield risk; tiered sensing balances labor against phenotypic completeness; staffing models present realistic trade-offs; expansion gating preserves optionality; and the algorithm scope avoids premature clinical claims. However, one critical missing dimension is participant retention and attrition modeling. The plan assumes 70–80% event yield but does not account for mid-stay dropout due to homesickness, discomfort in an unfamiliar environment, or regulatory/safety incidents that force early discharge—attrition rates of 15–25% are common in residential clinical research. Add to Lever 1 a fourth option: 'Implement a structured participant-experience protocol including daily brief phone calls to household contacts, mid-stay wellness check-ins, and a €20 bonus at day 7 and day 14 milestones to reduce dropout-driven unproductive admissions by ~8–12%.'

responses.strategic_rationale: This project navigates three fundamental tensions: (1) capturing rare naturalistic parasomnia events requires extended residential stays and unobtrusive sensing, yet the cost and operational complexity of residential monitoring demands strict gatekeeping on recruitment and facility expansion; (2) scientific rigor demands dual human scoring and algorithmic validation, but manual annotation throughput limits how many events can be processed and how quickly publications can emerge; (3) long-term sustainability depends on external funding contingencies and potential pharma partnerships, yet the academic mission requires full publication autonomy and methodological independence. The seven levers below address recruitment stringency vs. yield, technician workload vs. data completeness, sensing sophistication vs. operational burden, algorithm maturity vs. deployment risk, participant retention vs. safety thresholds, international collaboration vs. operational control, and post-hoc dataset reuse vs. privacy constraints—each forcing explicit choices about speed, cost, scope, and ethical boundaries.

levers.name: Phenotyping Rigor versus Event Capture Volume
levers.consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission diagnostic criteria (e.g., requiring polysomnographic evidence of parasomnia in screening, not just clinical history) reduce false recruitment and improve cohort homogeneity. Systemic: Tighter phenotyping delays enrollment by 3–6 weeks per participant but increases the proportion of admitted cases with at least one captured event from the baseline 70–80% to >90%, meaning fewer unproductive admissions and better sensitivity benchmarking for the event-triage algorithm. Strategic: This accelerates publication timelines and reduces the cost-per-validated-event, but narrows the applicability of phenotyping results to 'classic' presentation; atypical or subclinical parasomnias go undocumented, limiting generalizability.
options: Require documented polysomnographic evidence of parasomnia or abnormal arousal during pre-screening polysomnography, adjudicated by the PI before enrollment, accepting a 20–25% reduction in referral conversion and a 4–6 week pre-admission diagnostic window.

Accept clinical interview and collateral bed-partner history without antecedent polysomnography, permitting faster enrollment and capture of early-career or milder phenotypes, but tolerating 15–20% unproductive admissions and a higher false-positive rate in algorithmic screening.

Implement a two-tier pre-screening: rapid clinical triage (interview + questionnaire) for immediate admission to a 1-week 'diagnostic stay' with enhanced PSG montage on nights 1–3, followed by PI adjudication and either full enrollment or discharge, balancing speed and phenotypic clarity while doubling pre-enrollment costs.
levers.review_lever: Controls diagnostic specificity vs. recruitment pace and event yield. Weakness: The options fail to consider variation in parasomnia presentation across age, sex, and cultural background, risking a cohort skewed toward a single phenotype (e.g., young male sleepwalkers) and limiting the generalizability of longitudinal patterns.

levers.name: Technician Annotation Ceiling and Triage Algorithm Dependency
levers.consequences: Immediate: With 3 technicians rotating night shifts across 8 active suites, real-time annotation capacity is approximately 15–25 events per week if technicians must independently score and document each flagged episode. Systemic: Deploying a semi-automated event-triage tool that ranks candidate events by likelihood reduces manual review time per episode by 30–40%, permitting a ceiling of 35–50 events per week and enabling the study to process twice as many admissions. Strategic: Faster event throughput accelerates Aim 2 phenotyping and publication, but the algorithm must be trained on enough dual-scored human data to avoid systemic bias; if algorithm training lags, technicians will be bottlenecked waiting for algorithm recommendations, negating the throughput gain.
options: Maintain dual real-time annotation by technicians on all flagged events throughout the pilot and year one, accepting a 15–25 events-per-week ceiling and deliberately slow event accumulation, but ensuring algorithm training data is high-fidelity and unbiased from the outset.

Implement the semi-automated triage tool by month 6 year one using a training set of 200–300 manually scored events from the pilot cohort, permitting technicians to prioritize high-confidence events for dual scoring and deprioritize low-confidence candidates, trading off some low-event sensitivity for operational velocity.

Outsource post-hoc dual scoring to two independent sleep medicine specialists at a remote institution (e.g., Berlin) via secure asynchronous review of de-identified 60-second video-EEG clips, allowing technicians to focus solely on real-time safety and basic flagging, reducing their annotation workload by 50% but introducing a 1–2 week lag in event adjudication and a €80–120K year two cost.
levers.review_lever: Controls technician capacity constraints vs. algorithm maturity and event throughput. Weakness: The options fail to consider the training-data imbalance problem: if rare parasomnia events occur in only 70% of admitted participants, the training set may skew toward false positives from normal nocturnal behavior, causing the algorithm to over-flag and undo labor savings.

levers.name: Sensing Modality Footprint and Participant Burden Trade-off
levers.consequences: Immediate: The current tiered model (nightly dry-electrode EEG + mattress + infrared camera; enhanced PSG on 3 nights; escalation on event nights) balances safety and naturalism. Systemic: Adding continuous full PSG montage (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG every night) improves morphological classification accuracy by approximately 15–25% but increases equipment cost by €150–200K and technician setup time by 20 minutes per participant per night, compressing admissions throughput by 10–15% and raising per-participant direct costs by €800–1200. Strategic: The richer signal accelerates algorithm development but risks driving away participants concerned about electrode burden; conversely, minimizing nightly PSG preserves naturalism but may miss subclinical or morphologically atypical events, limiting phenotypic resolution.
options: Retain the current three-tier sensing model, accepting that morphological classification of non-captured events relies on real-time video-EEG observation by technicians rather than post-hoc algorithmic feature extraction, prioritizing participant recruitment and operational simplicity.

Deploy continuous full PSG montage on all nights for all participants from day one, front-loading equipment cost and setup burden to maximize signal fidelity and algorithm training data, expecting 10–12% lower enrollment velocity and higher dropouts due to electrode discomfort.

Implement a pragmatic hybrid: full PSG montage on nights 1, 4, 7, 14, 21, and final night (6 nights for a 2-week stay), capturing temporal variation and morphological nuance without nightly burden, requiring a software-defined PSG switching system and a 15% increase in technician cross-training on partial setup.
levers.review_lever: Controls sensing completeness vs. participant naturalness and operational scaling. Weakness: The options fail to consider individual heterogeneity in signal quality—some participants may have high-impedance scalp sites or poor mattress-sensor contact, rendering partial-PSG designs unreliable for specific individuals, necessitating mid-stay protocol amendments that complicate analysis.

levers.name: Algorithm Validation Framework and Clinical Deployment Ambition
levers.consequences: Immediate: The current plan benchmarks the event-triage tool against dual human scorer agreement, treating the algorithm as a decision-support aid, not an autonomous diagnostic. Systemic: Expanding the validation scope to include sensitivity/specificity tables across parasomnia subtypes and nocturnal movement thresholds adds 2–3 months to algorithm refinement and requires a holdout test set of 100+ independently adjudicated events, delaying year-two publications by one quarter but producing clinically actionable performance metrics. Strategic: Rigorous subtype-specific benchmarking positions the unit for a phase-two clinical utility trial and future dissemination to other sleep labs, but overstating algorithm performance invites regulatory scrutiny and claims of 'autonomous diagnosis' that violate the project's methodological mission.
options: Treat the event-triage algorithm as a technician-facing recommendation system: evaluate sensitivity and specificity only against the overall dual-scored dataset without subtype stratification, publish a methods paper with overall accuracy (e.g., 'Algorithm flags 85% of parasomnia events'), and reserve subtype-specific validation for a potential follow-on grant.

Implement rigorous subtype-specific validation: stratify the test set by parasomnia type (sleepwalking vs. confusional arousal vs. sleep terror vs. RBD mimics), report separate sensitivity/specificity for each, and require agreement between algorithm and dual scorers on event phenotype, enabling precise clinical recommendations but extending algorithm development into year three.

Conduct a prospective blind trial in year three: train the algorithm on year one and two data, then prospectively score 50 new incoming participants' night 1 data using only the algorithm (without technician pre-scoring), compare algorithm event lists against dual-scored reference standard in real time, and publish a 'clinical utility' paper, accepting the risk that algorithm performance degrades on new populations.
levers.review_lever: Controls algorithm scope and maturity claims vs. publication timeline and clinical readiness. Weakness: The options fail to consider the selection bias inherent in the training set—if the algorithm trains on events that technicians flag in real time, it will learn to replicate technician bias rather than discover parasomnia events de novo, inflating apparent performance.

levers.name: Participant Retention Protocols and Safety Threshold Calibration
levers.consequences: Immediate: The current plan caps stays at 10 weeks and tolerates 20% unproductive admissions before triggering recruitment review. Systemic: Introducing mid-stay safety debriefs (week 2 and week 4) and structured exit interviews asking participants to report discomfort, false alarms, or privacy concerns can lower dropout rates by 10–15% and identify equipment malfunctions or technician protocol drift early, reducing the effective unproductive admission rate to 12–15%. Strategic: Better retention increases event capture per enrolled participant, accelerating phenotyping; however, extending stays beyond 8 weeks consumes bed-nights at a cost of €1,600–2,400 per additional week, and participants with zero captured events may become demoralized, raising ethical concerns about informed consent and perceived benefit.
options: Implement structured safety and satisfaction surveys at weeks 2 and 4 of each stay, flag any participant reporting >1 false alarm per night or discomfort with specific sensors, empower technicians to offer real-time protocol adjustments (e.g., relocate electrodes, reduce camera angle) without PI approval, and document all amendments for post-hoc reliability assessment.

Establish a hard ceiling: if a participant produces zero events by week 6, conduct a PI-led review conversation (not a technician conversation) to explore whether continuation is medically safe and ethically justified; offer the option of discharge with full compensation, or extension for a final 2-week window with explicit re-consent, capping total stay at 8 weeks for zero-event participants.

Introduce a 'naturalism adaptation period' (week 1) during which comprehensive equipment and safety orientation occurs but advanced sensing (mattress sensors, escalation PSG setup) is not deployed; transition to full tiered sensing in week 2, allowing participants to physiologically habituate and reducing week 1–2 false-alarm rates by approximately 20% but delaying event capture analysis.
levers.review_lever: Controls early event detection vs. participant wellbeing and ethical justification for extended stays. Weakness: The options fail to consider cultural and individual differences in tolerance for prolonged monitoring and isolation; German participants may respond differently to 6–10 week stays than international referrals, and psychosocial factors (loneliness, fear of symptom absence) are not explicitly measured in any proposed safety threshold.

levers.name: Multi-Center Replication and Dataset Portability
levers.consequences: Immediate: The current plan is a single-site, single-PI operation in Bonn, limiting replication and generalizability. Systemic: Establishing a formal data-sharing and methods-replication agreement with one external sleep center (e.g., Munich, Berlin, or Austria) requires drafting common data dictionaries, harmonizing BIDS-compatible formats, and training external technicians on the Bonn protocol; this adds 3–4 months of coordination in year one but enables year-two recruitment of 10–15 participants at the external site, increasing event diversity and regional variation by ~25% and strengthening future grant proposals. Strategic: Multi-center data pools accelerate phenotyping and algorithm generalizability, but protocol fidelity becomes harder to maintain, inter-site variability in event capture may obscure true biological patterns, and data governance complexity rises, requiring explicit data use agreements and central adjudication procedures.
options: Maintain a single-site operation in Bonn throughout the 3-year project, preserving protocol homogeneity and PI authority, publishing results as 'Bonn Cohort' with clear acknowledgment of single-site limitations, and positioning multi-center replication as a phase-two objective.

Establish a formal collaborative agreement with one external sleep center by month 3 year one, sharing the complete protocol manual, sensor specifications, and annotation guidelines; recruit and train external technicians to mirror Bonn procedures, with all external-site PSG data re-scored by the Bonn dual-scorer team via remote secure review, adding €120–150K year-one costs for coordination and year-two cross-site adjudication.

Propose a consortium structure: convene a founding consortium of 3–4 sleep centers in German-speaking Europe (Germany, Austria, Switzerland) under a shared governance board, each site runs a parallel recruitment and monitoring arm, and all data feed into a central harmonized database with distributed dual-scoring (each site scores its own data; external data reviewed by a rotating consortium reviewer pair), enabling rapid multi-center replication but requiring €30–50K administrative infrastructure investment and quarterly governance meetings.
levers.review_lever: Controls scientific replicability and algorithm generalizability vs. operational complexity and governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider the logistical barriers to multi-site harmonization: recruitment pathways, participant compensation norms, and ethics board review timelines differ across German-speaking countries, and technical heterogeneity in sensor hardware calibration may introduce uncontrolled variability that undermines the benefits of larger sample sizes.

levers.name: Pharma Partnership Scope and Publication Independence Boundaries
levers.consequences: Immediate: The current plan treats a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside, offering a 30-day pre-publication comment window but retaining full academic autonomy. Systemic: A more formal partnership (e.g., cost-sharing on equipment or participant compensation in exchange for priority access to de-identified datasets) could inject €300–500K in year-one and year-two funding, reducing DFG and University of Bonn dependency by 20–25%, but introduces contractual obligations for data-sharing timelines and may create implicit pressure to frame results favorably toward the partner's therapeutic interests (e.g., if the partner is developing a novel RBD treatment, early negative efficacy signals in the exploratory RBD arm might be suppressed). Strategic: Pharma funding accelerates infrastructure buildout and boosts operational sustainability, but academic trust and methodological impartiality—central to publication credibility and future NIH/EU funding—are at risk if the partnership is perceived as compromising independence.
options: Decline any formal pharma partnership; fund the project solely through DFG and University of Bonn mechanisms, preserving complete publication and methodological autonomy, accepting the risk that if DFG year-three conditional funding is withheld, the unit may face mid-project contraction or closure.

Negotiate a 'data partnership' with one established sleep/neurology pharma company: they provide €250K year-one and €200K year-two in exchange for non-exclusive access to de-identified physiological datasets and a seat (non-voting) on the external advisory board; retain unilateral publication rights but commit to a 60-day pre-publication comment window, allowing the partner to raise technical concerns without veto authority.

Structure a 'clinical trial-support' partnership: the pharma partner funds a parallel ancillary protocol testing one of their investigational drugs in a subset of 15–20 RBD participants from the study, with the pharma company bearing all ancillary trial costs; keep the core Bonn parasomnia cohort and all publications completely independent, but leverage the ancillary revenue to offset core facility costs, adding complexity but protecting core-study autonomy.
levers.review_lever: Controls funding stability and operational scale vs. publication independence and academic reputation. Weakness: The options fail to consider reputational asymmetry: if a pharma partnership is later revealed to have influenced event-triage algorithm design or phenotyping interpretation, retrospective scientific scrutiny could invalidate publications retroactively, whereas a fully independent project withstands such challenges, and the long-term cost of lost credibility may exceed short-term funding gains.

responses.summary: These seven levers are well-balanced across operational, scientific, and governance dimensions, forcing explicit trade-offs between speed (phenotyping rigor, algorithm maturity), cost (sensing modality, multi-center expansion, pharma funding), scope (participant retention, dataset reuse), and methodological independence (algorithm validation, publication autonomy). However, a critical gap exists: none of the levers address the temporal sequencing and pacing of recruitment relative to facility preparation and technician training readiness. The plan assumes the facility opens in Q3 2026 and enrolls the pilot cohort smoothly, but real-world renovation delays, ethics board approval lag times, and technician onboarding are not explicitly constrained. **Prescription:** Add an eighth lever—'Pilot Phasing and Pre-Operational Readiness Checkpoints'—with options spanning: (1) a full hard-launch model (complete renovation and staff hiring before any enrollment), (2) a rolling pilot model (partial admission of 5–8 participants during month 4–5 of renovation to validate sensor and safety systems in parallel), and (3) a staged soft-launch model (months 1–2 limited to diagnostic stays and healthy controls only, advancing to full NREM parasomnia admissions in month 3 once technician protocols are validated). This would anchor the strategic choices in realistic calendar milestones and force explicit prioritization of de-risking operations versus maximizing year-one event capture.

responses.strategic_rationale: This parasomnia research facility navigates three fundamental tensions: (1) naturalistic behavior capture versus clinical-grade instrumentation—residential ambiance suppresses pathology but tiered sensing must reliably detect rare events; (2) operational sustainability versus discovery scope—limited team capacity and event yield unpredictability threaten enrollment targets and publication viability; (3) data utility versus privacy constraints—video footage is clinically essential but legally untransferable, constraining repository sharing and pharma collaboration models. The levers below address recruitment robustness under yield uncertainty, technician workload optimization in multi-suite operations, algorithmic automation boundaries that respect human oversight, facilities scaling tied to measurable operational maturity, and partnership models that preserve academic independence while securing contingency funding. These levers are critical because recruitment, staffing, and algorithm scope directly determine whether the facility achieves its 50–70 participant target and produces publishable Aim 2 and Aim 3 outcomes within budget constraints.

levers.name: Enrollment Duration Flexibility and Participant Throughput Target
levers.consequences: Immediate: Allowing variable stay lengths (2–10 weeks) and rolling admissions decouples enrollment from calendar quarters. Systemic: Throughput scales 15–25% depending on mean occupancy—tighter 2–4 week gates with rolling admission yield ~36–42 annualized admissions across 8 suites versus fixed 6-week cohorts yielding ~24–28, but variable lengths increase scheduling complexity and reduce predictable staffing demand. Strategic: This trade-off between enrollment velocity and operational predictability determines whether the facility meets its 50–70 target within 24 months or faces recruitment extension and delayed publications; missing the target by month 18 triggers external review and potential funding contingencies.
options: Implement rolling admissions with dynamic 2–4 week target stays for participants reaching event-capture thresholds, extending only zero-event cases to 8–10 weeks, with a standing protocol review every 6 months to adjust gates based on actual event yield and suite occupancy data.

Adopt cohort-based enrollment in 6-week fixed blocks synchronized to quarterly DFG reporting cycles, accepting lower throughput in exchange for predictable staffing and simplified coordination with the external advisory board and ethics oversight.

Establish a hybrid model pairing rapid 2–week screening admissions for high-likelihood candidates (drawn from sleep clinic referrals with documented bed-partner corroboration) followed by conditional extended stays only for confirmed zero-event cases, gating extension approval through the PI and study coordinator to balance throughput against false negatives.
levers.review_lever: Controls participant throughput (speed-to-enrollment) vs. operational complexity (staffing predictability and suite utilization). Weakness: The options fail to consider whether variable stay length affects inter-rater reliability scoring timelines or creates bottlenecks in the independent scoring pipeline when two raters must complete dual annotation within a fixed window after discharge.

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Maturation Cadence and Technician Annotation Role
levers.consequences: Immediate: Technician real-time flagging combined with offline algorithmic ranking creates two parallel triage streams. Systemic: A mature semi-automated tool reducing manual review burden by 30–50% yields 2–3 FTE-equivalent relief by year three, but premature deployment (year-one algorithm trained on <25 participants) risks >15% false-negative rates, causing missed rare events and undermining Aim 2 phenotyping. Strategic: This lever determines whether the technician role evolves toward event response and safety monitoring (reducing burnout and turnover) or remains bottlenecked in annotation; delaying algorithm development risks staffing attrition while aggressive timelines risk published performance claims that cannot withstand peer review.
options: Front-load algorithm development in year one with a focused team (the computational neuroscientist and data engineer) using synthetic event libraries and transferred learning from public polysomnography datasets, iteratively refining on captured events with monthly validation cycles, aiming for a usable triage tool by month 12 that is positioned as research-stage rather than clinical-grade.

Defer substantive algorithm work until year two after accumulating 40–50 adjudicated events from the pilot cohort, employing a conservative approach that trains only on institution-specific data, accepting higher year-one technician workload in exchange for robust year-two and year-three performance claims.

Establish a dual-track model where a junior research engineer develops algorithmic ranking pipelines in parallel with technician annotation, publishing yearly methodological preprints documenting algorithm performance against growing datasets without claiming clinical utility, positioning the output as a public research tool for community benchmarking.
levers.review_lever: Controls algorithm maturity ambition (performance claims) vs. data sufficiency (training sample size and generalizability). Weakness: The options fail to consider the downstream cost of independent scoring—if algorithm deployment reduces flagged events by 40%, the dual-rater scoring workload may underutilize scoring budget and delay publication timelines, or conversely, over-flagging by an immature algorithm may overwhelm scoring capacity and create backlogs.

levers.name: Facility Safety Clearance Criteria and Participant Exclusion Boundary
levers.consequences: Immediate: Expanding exclusion checklist scope (e.g., adding severe untreated hypertension, active sleep apnea comorbidity, or borderline wandering risk) tightens enrollment. Systemic: Stricter criteria reduce incident probability by ~20–30% but shrink eligible referral pool by an estimated 25–40%, lowering absolute enrollment and event capture counts; conversely, looser criteria maintain throughput but increase safety incidents (false alarms, minor injuries) by ~10–15% and raise institutional liability and ethics committee scrutiny. Strategic: The balance between safety conservatism and research feasibility determines whether the facility achieves its 50–70 target without triggering ethics reviews that halt admissions; a single serious preventable incident (e.g., fall during monitored parasomnia in an insufficiently screened participant) could terminate the program and require 6–12 month remediation.
options: Adopt a narrow protocol restricting admission to NREM parasomnia cases with documented prior PSG confirmation and explicit bed-partner attestation of non-injurious episodes, excluding any participant with prior emergency care for parasomnia-related injury, untreated sleep apnea, or hypertension >160/100, accepting ~50–60 eligible participants over 3 years.

Implement a structured graduated-risk framework where borderline cases (e.g., mild hypertension, remote sleep apnea not currently treated) undergo targeted screening PSG before admission, with enhanced suite assignments (camera angle, proximity to night technician workstation) and real-time cardiorespiratory monitoring during enhanced nights, enabling inclusion of ~70–85 participants while preserving safety margins.

Establish tiered enrollment pathways where tier-one participants meet strict exclusion criteria and proceed directly to residential study, while tier-two participants undergo 1–2 week outpatient assessment (home actigraphy and sleep diary) with conditional admission only if no safety flags emerge, optimizing enrollment volume while distributing safety evaluation burden.
levers.review_lever: Controls safety conservatism (incident avoidance) vs. research scope (enrollment feasibility and phenotyping completeness). Weakness: The options fail to consider how exclusion stringency affects matched control recruitment—if NREM participants require extensive pre-screening, control matching on age and sex alone may introduce unmeasured health-status confounding that weakens Aim 2 phenotyping validity.

levers.name: Independent Scoring Workforce Model and Dual-Rater Reliability Assurance
levers.consequences: Immediate: Hiring two dedicated full-time sleep technologists for dual-blind scoring ensures independent assessment and high inter-rater reliability (target >0.75 Cohen's kappa). Systemic: Full-time scorers cost approximately €100–120K annually with benefits (~€300K over 3 years) and require consistent event volume to remain productive; underutilization or backlogs delay publication timelines by 2–4 months. Conversely, on-demand contract scoring (freelance trained technologists working event-by-event) reduces fixed overhead by 40% but introduces quality variability and ~2-week turnaround delays. Strategic: Scoring infrastructure directly affects publication velocity—delayed dual-rating blocks Aim 2 manuscript submission; choosing cost efficiency over speed risks missing 24-month publication targets and undermining grant renewal credibility.
options: Hire two full-time sleep technologists with PSG scoring certification, embedded in the Bonn facility with cross-training on parasomnia morphology, tasked with dual-blind scoring of all enhanced-night and escalation-night PSG segments within 5 working days of acquisition, with a published inter-rater reliability report in every manuscript.

Establish a distributed on-demand network of 4–6 trained freelance scorers recruited from German sleep medicine centers and universities, each compensated per-event at €15–25 with centralized quality control reviews by the PI, accepting 10–14 day turnaround and lower consistency in exchange for 30% cost reduction and flexible scaling.

Develop a hybrid model pairing one full-time in-house scorer managing the pipeline and quality assurance with two part-time (0.5 FTE) contract scorers for event overflow, balancing cost predictability (€180–200K over 3 years) against medium-speed turnaround (7–10 days) and enabling on-demand scaling during high-event periods without fixed overhead bloat.
levers.review_lever: Controls reliability assurance (dual-rating rigor) vs. cost and scheduling flexibility (workforce adaptability to variable event volume). Weakness: The options fail to consider whether scoring expertise in parasomnia-specific morphology (versus general PSG competency) is learnable within months or requires 1–2 years of mentorship, potentially extending ramp-up timelines and delaying publishable inter-rater data.

levers.name: Data Repository and Pharma Partner Access Negotiation
levers.consequences: Immediate: Depositing de-identified physiological data in a public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro or XNAT) without raw video enables broad research access and citation credit. Systemic: Public deposition increases dataset reuse and citations by an estimated 40–60% within 3 years and attracts secondary analyses that boost publication count; however, pharma partnership negotiations often demand exclusive pre-publication access to raw datasets for competitive advantage, creating a 12–18 month conflict between open-science release and pharma-funded expansion plans. Strategic: This lever determines whether the facility positions as an open-research asset aligned with German data-sharing norms and DFG expectations (supporting Aim 3 reproducibility) or captures pharma upside through data exclusivity agreements that subsidize year-three expansion but risk reputational tension with the academic community.
options: Commit unconditionally to public deposition of all de-identified physiological data and event-scored annotations within 12 months of participant discharge, explicitly excluding any pharma partner exclusivity clauses, prioritizing academic independence and alignment with DFG funding conditions at the potential cost of pharma partnership leverage.

Negotiate a tiered pharma access model where the academic team retains full publication rights and commits public deposition after 24 months (not 12), allowing pharma a first-access window of 18 months for internal analysis but no exclusivity over published data, enabling partnership upside while preserving eventual open science intent.

Structure pharma engagement as a data-licensing relationship where processed algorithmic outputs (event-triage predictions, temporal clustering summaries) and aggregate phenotyping results are available to partners under data use agreements, while raw physiological signals remain institution-controlled and subject to separate open-science publication pipelines, compartmentalizing commercial and academic interests.
levers.review_lever: Controls academic openness (public data deposit and broad reuse) vs. partnership revenue (pharma exclusivity leverage and contingency funding). Weakness: The options fail to consider whether pharma partners prioritize algorithm training data (which requires raw signals) versus finished decision-support tools, and whether licensing access to processed outputs is commercially attractive enough to offset the loss of raw-data exclusivity that firms typically demand.

levers.name: Suite Expansion Trigger Criteria and Facility Scaling Timeline
levers.consequences: Immediate: Maintaining 8 suites for 18 months generates baseline operational metrics (staffing ratios, safety incident rates, annotation throughput, event yield). Systemic: Expanding to 12 suites (contingent on proof of safe operations and 40+ captured events) increases annualized throughput by ~50% and accelerates publication cadence, but requires an additional €200–250K renovation budget and 1.5 FTE additional technician coverage (~€90K annually); delayed expansion postpones Aim 2 phenotyping completeness by 6–12 months. Strategic: The expansion decision is binary—commit to a 12-month sustained-operations milestone by month 18 or formally scale back enrollment ambitions; neither deferring the decision nor pursuing unplanned expansion mid-year is operationally feasible given staffing lead times.
options: Set an explicit 18-month gate requiring proof of (1) zero serious safety incidents, (2) >80% occupancy of 8 suites averaged quarterly, (3) ≥40 independently adjudicated events captured, and (4) positive ethics committee feedback, with board approval for expansion to 12 suites only if all four criteria are met by month 18, triggering renovation and hiring immediately thereafter.

Defer expansion consideration until month 24, allowing full year-two enrollment to mature and event-yield projections to stabilize, accepting slower overall throughput but reducing financial and staffing risk; expansion decision then reflects 18 months of operational data and enables more confident year-three planning.

Establish a staged pre-commitment where the facility secures option agreements for suite renovation and technician hiring by month 12, allowing the team to begin pre-vetting candidates and contractor quotes without budget obligation, with a go/no-go decision at month 16 based on interim metrics, enabling a 2-month lead time for renovation and staffing if expansion is triggered.
levers.review_lever: Controls expansion pace (throughput acceleration) vs. operational maturity (validation of safe and sustainable 8-suite baseline). Weakness: The options fail to consider whether the same night-shift technician team can safely supervise 12 suites (a ~50% workload increase during event-rich nights) or whether psychological and physical fatigue will degrade response times and annotation quality despite larger headcount, requiring a deeper burnout and safety-culture analysis.

levers.name: Matched Control Recruitment Strategy and Baseline Phenotyping Scope
levers.consequences: Immediate: Recruiting age- and sex-matched healthy controls adds 15–20 participants but requires identical 2–8 week residential monitoring without any expectation of captured parasomnia events. Systemic: Controls provide essential baseline false-positive rates from dry-electrode EEG, mattress sensors, and infrared cameras during normal sleep, reducing algorithmic false-positive risk by ~20–30%; however, control enrollment and housing costs approximately €50–80K (supplies, meals, compensation at same €80/night rate) and yields zero event-capture payoff, straining year-two budget utilization. Strategic: The control arm is methodologically essential for Aim 3 (algorithm benchmarking against normal nocturnal behavior) but operationally costly; omitting controls weakens peer-review credibility for algorithm specificity claims, while including controls risks budget overruns if case enrollment lags and control slots cannot be reallocated.
options: Enroll matched controls proportionally throughout the study (e.g., one control per two cases), distributed across year one and year two, with identical monitoring protocols and synchronized scoring, accepting elevated year-two costs in exchange for robust Aim 3 algorithmic false-positive baselines publishable in the primary methods validation paper.

Defer all control enrollment to a dedicated year-three phase after case enrollment and event capture are complete, reducing year-one and year-two budget pressure and allowing the team to retarget control recruitment based on learned participant preferences and optimal sensor configuration, trading publication timeline (Aim 3 deferred to year 3+) for financial flexibility.

Implement a lean control arm where 8–10 controls undergo 3–5 day abbreviated stays focused on instrumentation validation (sensor signal quality, camera and microphone performance) rather than full 2–8 week monitoring, capturing false-positive baseline data efficiently and reducing costs by 40%, accepting reduced statistical power for Aim 3 specificity claims.
levers.review_lever: Controls phenotyping comprehensiveness (robust Aim 3 algorithm false-positive data) vs. budget efficiency (opportunity cost of control enrollment during case recruitment uncertainty). Weakness: The options fail to consider whether controls and cases require simultaneous or staggered suite assignment, and whether control scheduling flexibility (versus case scheduling driven by referral timing) can be exploited to optimize suite utilization across both cohorts without cross-contamination of sleep environment.

responses.summary: These seven levers address the core operational tensions facing the facility: enrollment velocity and throughput predictability (Lever 1), technician workload and algorithm maturity timing (Lever 2), safety conservatism versus research scope (Lever 3), scoring reliability and cost structure (Lever 4), academic openness versus pharma partnership upside (Lever 5), facility expansion gating and scaling risk (Lever 6), and control recruitment cost-effectiveness (Lever 7). The levers are well-balanced across recruitment, staffing, algorithm scope, facilities, partnerships, and methodological rigor; they challenge core assumptions (e.g., whether variable stay lengths are compatible with dual-rater timelines, or whether the same technician team can safely manage 12 suites). A critical missing dimension is the *participant experience and retention pathway*—the levers address enrollment and event capture but not retention strategies, drop-out prevention, or long-term participant satisfaction, which directly affect whether the facility achieves its 50–70 target and maintains referral network loyalty. Add to Lever 1 a strategic option addressing participant retention bonuses, mid-stay supportive contacts, or post-discharge follow-up incentives that maintain engagement and reduce withdrawal-driven unproductive admissions.

levers.name: Recruitment Entry Pathway and Diagnostic Stringency
levers.consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission screening (specialist interview + prior PSG + structured exclusion checklist) reduces on-site misadmission and participant frustration, but delays enrollment by 4–6 weeks per cohort. Systemic: A 2–week gating period increases staffing utilization (fewer wasted bed-nights on misclassified participants) and improves event yield from ~65% to ~78%, reducing the unproductive-admission ceiling from 20% to ~12%; this shifts 3–5 participants per year from zero-event discharges to event-generating admissions. Strategic: Frontloading diagnostic certainty enables rapid scientific publications and boosts grant-review credibility, but slower enrollment may compress year-two cohort recruitment, creating calendar risk if DFG funding is delayed.
options: Implement a two-stage pathway with a mandatory videoconsult and prior-PSG review before formal enrollment, scheduling only confirmed candidates to minimize on-site diagnostic ambiguity.

Accept clinic referrals with self-reported parasomnia history and perform diagnostic confirmation during the first 3 nights in-residence, discharging participants who lack captured events by day 4 and recycling those bed-slots to reduce vacancy.

Recruit 40% of participants from the Deutsche Gesellschaft network using a distributed phone-interview checklist administered by regional neurologists, circumventing University Hospital bottlenecks and enabling parallel enrollment pipelines.
levers.review: Controls diagnostic precision vs. enrollment velocity. Weakness: The options fail to account for bed-partner availability as a confounding variable—participants lacking corroborating household history may have legitimate parasomnias but be systematically excluded, biasing the final cohort toward cohabiting individuals.

levers.name: Tiered Sensing Architecture and Technician Annotation Load
levers.consequences: Immediate: Nightly dry-electrode EEG headbands + mattress sensors + infrared cameras generate 500+ GB per participant per week; dual-rater manual annotation of all continuous data requires 8–12 hours per night of technician/rater time, bottlenecking Aim 3 algorithm development. Systemic: Deploying scheduled enhanced PSG only on nights 1, mid-stay, and final (plus escalation nights) reduces total PSG montage-nights to ~30% of stay-nights, cutting annotation labor by ~55%, but this sparse sampling may miss infrequent event morphology variants, lowering algorithm generalization by an estimated 12–18%. Strategic: Conservative full PSG saturates the annotation pipeline and delays algorithm publication by 6+ months; adaptive sparse PSG accelerates time-to-publication but risks underfitting the event-triage model to rare phenotypes not captured on scheduled nights.
options: Acquire continuous full PSG on all nights for the first 15 participants (pilot cohort), generating complete annotation dataset for algorithm training, then transition to sparse PSG for subsequent cohorts once baseline morphology is characterized.

Implement nightly low-burden monitoring with structured human review flags: technician-tagged candidate windows undergo dual-rater PSG-free analysis (visual scoring from video + headband EEG + ambient audio), advancing to full PSG only when inter-rater agreement reaches <80%.

Deploy a trained graduate student to conduct real-time semi-automated triage on headband EEG and video streams each night, automatically triggering full PSG montage when spectral/movement signatures match prototypical parasomnia patterns, with human review reserved for algorithm-uncertain events.
levers.review: Controls annotation labor vs. phenotypic completeness. Weakness: The options fail to distinguish between annotation cost for Aim 2 (phenotyping) and Aim 3 (algorithm development)—a sparse PSG schedule may suffice for characterization but starve the algorithm of training examples, creating a hidden trade-off between publication speed and model robustness.

levers.name: Staffing Model and Night Coverage Redundancy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Covering 8 active suites nightly requires a minimum of 3 technicians on rotating 5-day shifts to ensure safe response to events; a full-time headcount of 4 technicians provides a 33% safety margin and absorbs vacation/illness. Systemic: Staffing a 3-person core with on-call backup shifts annual personnel costs by ~€280K (vs. 4-person core), but on-call deployment adds ~15% overtime and reduces annotation consistency (on-call staff are less familiar with participants and sensor-system quirks), lowering inter-rater reliability by an estimated 4–6 percentage points. Strategic: Lean staffing (3 FTE) preserves budget headroom for algorithm development and expansion; redundant staffing (4 FTE) ensures consistent data quality and reduces burnout-driven staff turnover, but forecloses year-three expansion if DFG funding plateaus.
options: Hire a full-time 4-person technician pool with formal night-shift rotation (5 nights per week per technician, ~2 weekends per month), eliminating on-call dependency and establishing continuity; absorb the €280K cost by reducing participant compensation from €80 to €65 per night or accepting deferred expansion.

Deploy a core 3-person team supplemented by a rotating pool of 2 trained undergraduate research assistants paid hourly at €16/hour, scheduled on-call for events; delegate annotation to a separate 2-person dedicated scoring cohort working day-shift, decoupling night response from daytime analysis.

Establish a formal collaboration with University Hospital Bonn's sleep technician training program, embedding 1–2 graduate-level students in the unit for 6-month rotations, providing them credit toward their clinical certification while supply-chaining technician labor at zero marginal cost after initial training.
levers.review: Controls staffing stability vs. personnel budget. Weakness: The options fail to address the hidden cost of turnover—a lean on-call model may incur 20–30% annual technician replacement, driving retraining costs and loss of institutional knowledge that exceeds the apparent budget savings.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Gating and Capacity Planning
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deferring expansion from 8 to 12 suites until month 12–18 (after pilot validation) reduces pre-operational capital risk; the 4-suite buffer provides insurance against design flaws without overbuilding. Systemic: Maintaining 8 suites for year one enrolls ~20 participants at 70-day average stay; adding 4 suites in month 18 increases year-two capacity to ~35 participants (a 75% increase), aligning with the year-two budget of €1.2M. However, premature expansion to 12 suites in month 6 could increase year-two enrollment to ~50–55 participants, improving event yield statistics by ~22%, but risks property-lease penalties and renovation cost overruns if quality issues emerge. Strategic: Conservative expansion minimizes sunk costs and preserves strategic optionality; aggressive expansion front-loads data collection and accelerates publication timeline, but betting infrastructure capital on unproven operational assumptions may consume the year-three conditional budget prematurely.
options: Gate expansion to month 18, conditional on achieving 80% data-quality score (inter-rater PSG reliability >85%, <8% false-alarm rate, <10% unproductive admissions); fund 4-suite renovation from year-two budget only if milestones are met.

Initiate 4-suite build-out in month 6 using a deferred-payment lease structure, deferring full occupancy to month 12; this front-loads infrastructure cost (~€180K) but enables gradual staffing ramp and captures 5–10 additional participants in year two.

Partner with University Hospital Bonn to lease 4 clinical sleep-medicine beds for overflow monitoring during peak enrollment windows (e.g., months 12–18), avoiding permanent facility expansion and maintaining flexibility to pivot if event yields prove lower than 70%.
levers.review: Controls capital risk vs. enrollment momentum. Weakness: The options fail to model the interaction between facility size, staff familiarity, and data-quality consistency—a rapid expansion to 12 suites may dilute technician expertise across more participants, degrading inter-rater reliability and offsetting the enrollment gain.

levers.name: Data Sharing and Repository Alignment Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Depositing de-identified physiological data (EEG, EOG, EMG, actigraphy, ambient sensors) in a BIDS-compliant repository (OpenNeuro or PhysioNet) enables secondary analysis and algorithmic benchmarking by external groups. Systemic: Public data sharing attracts ~15–25 external downloads per dataset per year and typically generates 2–4 citation-linked downstream publications within 24 months; this amplifies research impact (estimated 1.5–2× citation multiplier) but requires 80–120 hours of documentation effort per publication cycle and raises de-identification risk if sensor streams contain subtle privacy leakage (e.g., acoustic signatures revealing identity). Strategic: Open-data positioning strengthens grant renewals and establishes the unit as a community resource, but bedside-video privacy constraints limit the most naturalistic modality, potentially undermining algorithm generalization if external groups train on physiology alone.
options: Deposit complete physiological datasets (EEG, video-free) in OpenNeuro under CC0 licensing, with full BIDS metadata and annotated event csvs, enabling external algorithm benchmarking and maximizing citations; budget 100 hours year two for documentation.

Implement a tiered access model: de-identified physiology available under CC-BY open license, but video-linked datasets (optical flow, pose estimates without facial pixels) restricted to Data Use Agreements; this preserves privacy while enabling external algorithm development on a reduced feature set.

Restrict public sharing to an annual 'curated release' containing 10–15 fully annotated, de-identified participant records, managing privacy risk and documentation burden while still supporting 2–3 external collaborations per year through direct Data Use Agreements.
levers.review: Controls data access vs. privacy surface. Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory landscape—DPA compliance and GDPR Article 89 (research exemptions) require legal review regardless of repository choice, adding 8–10 weeks of upfront work that is not budgeted in the team's timeline.

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development Scope and Clinical Deployment Ambition
levers.consequences: Immediate: A benchmarking-only algorithm (Aim 3 as designed) produces sensitivity/specificity metrics against dual human scoring, enabling peer-reviewed publication in methods journals and reducing manual review burden from 8 hours/night to ~3 hours/night (by prioritizing probable events). Systemic: Advancing to a 'clinical-deployment-ready' algorithm (prospective validation against clinician interpretation, internal checklist review) demands 200+ additional hours of validation work and a 6–month extension beyond month 36, but increases algorithm adoption by hospital sleep labs, generating €50–150K in consultation revenue by year four. However, overstatement of algorithm maturity invites regulatory scrutiny and reputational risk if external groups benchmark the model and report failures on underrepresented phenotypes. Strategic: Conservative benchmarking-only scope aligns with the early-stage research mission and avoids premature clinical claims; deployment-ready framing could accelerate pharmaceutical partnerships (raising year-three revenue by ~€200K), but risks mission creep and regulatory entanglement that compromises academic independence.
options: Limit Aim 3 to a benchmarking study: train a semi-automated event triage on year-one/two data, report sensitivity/specificity against dual human scorers, and publish in a sleep medicine methods journal; explicitly avoid clinical-deployment language and recommend human oversight for any downstream use.

Develop the algorithm with prospective validation on a held-out test cohort (final 15–20 participants), comparing system rankings against clinician consensus, and submit a separate clinical-validation manuscript; position as 'promising for research use' and offer it freely to academic sleep labs as a screening tool.

Engineer the algorithm for ease-of-use deployment in hospital PSG systems via a DICOM plugin and REST API, document validation against multiple cohorts, and license it to sleep-medicine software vendors for a milestone-based revenue share; this accelerates algorithm reach but introduces vendor dependencies and marketing pressures that may dilute academic publishing.
levers.review: Controls scientific scope vs. translational ambition. Weakness: The options fail to address the Goodhart's Law problem—once an algorithm is positioned as a decision-support tool, institutional use will shift from 'research benchmarking' to 'clinical triage,' inverting the incentive structure and eroding the algorithm's transparency.

levers.name: DFG Funding Contingency and Year-Three Sustainability Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Primary funding from DFG grants covers €3.5M of the €3.8M budget; a €300K internal University of Bonn allocation funds contingency and year-three conditional costs. Systemic: If DFG funding is delayed beyond month 3 or reduced below €2.8M, year-one operations must be sustained by University internal funds, which are typically reallocated annually and cannot be relied upon; a delay of 6+ months forces a 25% reduction in pilot enrollment (from 20 to 15 participants) or a 4-month slippage to month 7 start date, cutting year-one capture by ~2–3 events and deferring publication to month 30. Strategic: Securing pharmaceutical partnerships (optional per plan) as supplementary revenue (€150–300K per partner) de-risks year-three expansion and funds algorithm commercialization; however, dependence on pharma revenue incentivizes faster-than-prudent deployment and dilutes the unit's independence.
options: Frontload all discretionary spending (equipment procurement, participant recruitment marketing) into months 1–3 using committed internal funds, demonstrating operational viability before DFG funds arrive; this compresses cash-flow risk into a 90-day window.

Negotiate a formal letter of intent with the DFG program officer at month −3 (pre-submission), securing a binding funding commitment or a written contingency timeline; if DFG approval is delayed >4 months, activate a secondary application to European Sleep Research Society infrastructure grants.

Cultivate 2–3 pharmaceutical partnerships in parallel (e.g., orexin antagonists, antiepileptic candidates for parasomnia) by month 6, structuring contracts to yield €100–150K each by month 18 in exchange for observational outcome reporting (non-exclusive, with full academic publication rights); use pharma revenue to de-fund year-three contingency and preserve University resources.
levers.review: Controls institutional dependency vs. external alignment. Weakness: The options fail to model the reputational cost of pharmaceutical partnerships—even with retained publication rights, external perception of industry sponsorship may bias recruitment (participants may self-select for higher treatment expectations) and bias interpretation (reviewers may discount findings as industry-influenced).

levers.name: Phenotyping Rigor versus Event Capture Volume
levers.consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission diagnostic criteria (e.g., requiring polysomnographic evidence of parasomnia in screening, not just clinical history) reduce false recruitment and improve cohort homogeneity. Systemic: Tighter phenotyping delays enrollment by 3–6 weeks per participant but increases the proportion of admitted cases with at least one captured event from the baseline 70–80% to >90%, meaning fewer unproductive admissions and better sensitivity benchmarking for the event-triage algorithm. Strategic: This accelerates publication timelines and reduces the cost-per-validated-event, but narrows the applicability of phenotyping results to 'classic' presentation; atypical or subclinical parasomnias go undocumented, limiting generalizability.
options: Require documented polysomnographic evidence of parasomnia or abnormal arousal during pre-screening polysomnography, adjudicated by the PI before enrollment, accepting a 20–25% reduction in referral conversion and a 4–6 week pre-admission diagnostic window.

Accept clinical interview and collateral bed-partner history without antecedent polysomnography, permitting faster enrollment and capture of early-career or milder phenotypes, but tolerating 15–20% unproductive admissions and a higher false-positive rate in algorithmic screening.

Implement a two-tier pre-screening: rapid clinical triage (interview + questionnaire) for immediate admission to a 1-week 'diagnostic stay' with enhanced PSG montage on nights 1–3, followed by PI adjudication and either full enrollment or discharge, balancing speed and phenotypic clarity while doubling pre-enrollment costs.
levers.review: Controls diagnostic specificity vs. recruitment pace and event yield. Weakness: The options fail to consider variation in parasomnia presentation across age, sex, and cultural background, risking a cohort skewed toward a single phenotype (e.g., young male sleepwalkers) and limiting the generalizability of longitudinal patterns.

levers.name: Technician Annotation Ceiling and Triage Algorithm Dependency
levers.consequences: Immediate: With 3 technicians rotating night shifts across 8 active suites, real-time annotation capacity is approximately 15–25 events per week if technicians must independently score and document each flagged episode. Systemic: Deploying a semi-automated event-triage tool that ranks candidate events by likelihood reduces manual review time per episode by 30–40%, permitting a ceiling of 35–50 events per week and enabling the study to process twice as many admissions. Strategic: Faster event throughput accelerates Aim 2 phenotyping and publication, but the algorithm must be trained on enough dual-scored human data to avoid systemic bias; if algorithm training lags, technicians will be bottlenecked waiting for algorithm recommendations, negating the throughput gain.
options: Maintain dual real-time annotation by technicians on all flagged events throughout the pilot and year one, accepting a 15–25 events-per-week ceiling and deliberately slow event accumulation, but ensuring algorithm training data is high-fidelity and unbiased from the outset.

Implement the semi-automated triage tool by month 6 year one using a training set of 200–300 manually scored events from the pilot cohort, permitting technicians to prioritize high-confidence events for dual scoring and deprioritize low-confidence candidates, trading off some low-event sensitivity for operational velocity.

Outsource post-hoc dual scoring to two independent sleep medicine specialists at a remote institution (e.g., Berlin) via secure asynchronous review of de-identified 60-second video-EEG clips, allowing technicians to focus solely on real-time safety and basic flagging, reducing their annotation workload by 50% but introducing a 1–2 week lag in event adjudication and a €80–120K year two cost.
levers.review: Controls technician capacity constraints vs. algorithm maturity and event throughput. Weakness: The options fail to consider the training-data imbalance problem: if rare parasomnia events occur in only 70% of admitted participants, the training set may skew toward false positives from normal nocturnal behavior, causing the algorithm to over-flag and undo labor savings.

levers.name: Sensing Modality Footprint and Participant Burden Trade-off
levers.consequences: Immediate: The current tiered model (nightly dry-electrode EEG + mattress + infrared camera; enhanced PSG on 3 nights; escalation on event nights) balances safety and naturalism. Systemic: Adding continuous full PSG montage (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG every night) improves morphological classification accuracy by approximately 15–25% but increases equipment cost by €150–200K and technician setup time by 20 minutes per participant per night, compressing admissions throughput by 10–15% and raising per-participant direct costs by €800–1200. Strategic: The richer signal accelerates algorithm development but risks driving away participants concerned about electrode burden; conversely, minimizing nightly PSG preserves naturalism but may miss subclinical or morphologically atypical events, limiting phenotypic resolution.
options: Retain the current three-tier sensing model, accepting that morphological classification of non-captured events relies on real-time video-EEG observation by technicians rather than post-hoc algorithmic feature extraction, prioritizing participant recruitment and operational simplicity.

Deploy continuous full PSG montage on all nights for all participants from day one, front-loading equipment cost and setup burden to maximize signal fidelity and algorithm training data, expecting 10–12% lower enrollment velocity and higher dropouts due to electrode discomfort.

Implement a pragmatic hybrid: full PSG montage on nights 1, 4, 7, 14, 21, and final night (6 nights for a 2-week stay), capturing temporal variation and morphological nuance without nightly burden, requiring a software-defined PSG switching system and a 15% increase in technician cross-training on partial setup.
levers.review: Controls sensing completeness vs. participant naturalness and operational scaling. Weakness: The options fail to consider individual heterogeneity in signal quality—some participants may have high-impedance scalp sites or poor mattress-sensor contact, rendering partial-PSG designs unreliable for specific individuals, necessitating mid-stay protocol amendments that complicate analysis.

levers.name: Algorithm Validation Framework and Clinical Deployment Ambition
